skill.name: azure-quotas
skill.description: Check Azure quota limits, usage, region capacity, and quota increase workflows with Azure CLI guidance.
skill.summary: Validate Azure service quotas before deployment and plan capacity across regions.
skill.author: microsoft

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, URL, and blocker patterns, but review shows the skill is Azure quota documentation rather than executable malware. The external commands are legitimate Azure CLI examples, while the weak cryptography and system reconnaissance alerts are false positives. Publish with an operational warning because some commands can submit quota changes in an authenticated Azure subscription.

medium_findings.title: Azure CLI Command Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill provides many Azure CLI command examples. This is expected for quota management, but users should review commands before execution because they query subscription data and depend on authenticated Azure context.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files contain direct Azure CLI examples for quota list, show, and status commands. The commands are legitimate administrative workflows and do not show malicious intent or command injection.

medium_findings.title: Quota Increase Commands Can Change Cloud State
medium_findings.description: The skill documents quota update and create workflows. These commands can submit Azure quota changes, so an operator should confirm the subscription, region, resource name, and requested value first.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples explicitly include az quota update and az quota create. This is a real operational risk, but it aligns with the skill purpose and requires Azure permissions.

medium_findings.title: Shell Command Substitution In Example Script
medium_findings.description: The regional comparison example uses shell command substitution to capture Azure CLI output. This is normal shell scripting, but user supplied region, resource, and subscription values should be validated before use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is present in a Bash example that calls az quota commands. It is not malicious, but careless interpolation can cause operational mistakes in copied scripts.

low_findings.title: Trusted Microsoft Links Are Benign Network References
low_findings.description: The static network findings point to Azure Portal and Microsoft Learn links. These are expected documentation references and no credential exfiltration endpoint was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All reviewed URLs point to Microsoft Azure Portal or Microsoft Learn. I found no evidence of external collection, tracking, or non-Microsoft network destinations.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The high static crypto alerts match ordinary documentation text and Azure quota terminology, not cryptographic code. No hashing, encryption, or password handling logic was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are markdown descriptions, command tables, or Azure error names. There is no cryptographic implementation or recommendation to use weak algorithms.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Alerts Are Azure Quota Queries
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged quota operation and request status examples as reconnaissance. The context is Azure quota administration, not local system discovery or covert inventory collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands query Microsoft.Quota request status and provider operations. This is expected Azure administration behavior with no stealth or host reconnaissance evidence.

dangerous_patterns.title: Azure CLI Command Execution Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill provides many Azure CLI command examples. This is expected for quota management, but users should review commands before execution because they query subscription data and depend on authenticated Azure context.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The files contain direct Azure CLI examples for quota list, show, and status commands. The commands are legitimate administrative workflows and do not show malicious intent or command injection.

dangerous_patterns.title: Quota Increase Commands Can Change Cloud State
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents quota update and create workflows. These commands can submit Azure quota changes, so an operator should confirm the subscription, region, resource name, and requested value first.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command examples explicitly include az quota update and az quota create. This is a real operational risk, but it aligns with the skill purpose and requires Azure permissions.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Substitution In Example Script
dangerous_patterns.description: The regional comparison example uses shell command substitution to capture Azure CLI output. This is normal shell scripting, but user supplied region, resource, and subscription values should be validated before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is present in a Bash example that calls az quota commands. It is not malicious, but careless interpolation can cause operational mistakes in copied scripts.

content.value_statement: Azure quota limits can block deployments and delay regional planning. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Azure CLI quota checks, usage review, and increase requests.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to install and use the Azure CLI quota extension.
Shows how to list quota limits for Azure providers and regions.
Guides comparison of quota usage across candidate deployment regions.
Documents quota increase requests and request status checks.
Clarifies quota resource name discovery with localized values.
Identifies fallback documentation when a provider lacks quota API support.
limitations: Requires an authenticated Azure CLI session with suitable subscription access.
Does not guarantee quota increase approval or regional resource availability.
Some Azure providers do not support the quota API commands.
Command examples must be adapted before use in production subscriptions.

use_cases.title: Validate Deployment Capacity
use_cases.description: Check current quota usage and limits before deploying virtual machines, networking, or container resources.

use_cases.title: Compare Candidate Regions
use_cases.description: Review available quota across several Azure regions before selecting a deployment location.

use_cases.title: Prepare Quota Increase Requests
use_cases.description: Find the correct quota resource name and plan a request with a clear target value.

prompt_templates.title: Check One Quota
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me check the Azure quota and current usage for one resource provider in one region.

prompt_templates.title: Find The Resource Name
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through finding the correct quota resource name for an Azure resource type.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Regions
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me compare available Azure quota across eastus, eastus2, westus2, and centralus.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Increase Request
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me prepare a safe Azure quota increase request and verify the request status afterward.

output_examples.input: Check whether East US has enough DSv3 family vCPU quota for a 40 vCPU deployment.
output_examples.output: The response explains how to list the quota, read current usage, calculate remaining capacity, and decide whether to request more quota.

output_examples.input: Compare compute quota in East US, East US 2, and West US 2.
output: A regional comparison plan with the subscription scope for each region.
A capacity calculation method for each quota result.
A recommendation to choose a region only after confirming current usage.

output_examples.input: I received BadRequest from the quota API for Cosmos DB.
output_examples.output: The response explains that the provider may not support quota commands and points to service-specific Azure limits documentation.

best_practices: Run read-only quota checks before any quota update or create command.
Confirm subscription, provider, region, and quota resource name before execution.
Keep a capacity buffer and document the source of each quota result.
anti_patterns: Assuming REST API No Limit values mean unlimited Azure capacity.
Copying quota update commands into production without reviewing target values.
Guessing quota resource names from ARM resource types without discovery.

faq.question: Does this skill execute Azure commands by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides command guidance that a user or agent may choose to run.

faq.question: What Azure permission is needed for read-only quota checks?
faq.answer: Reader access is usually enough to view quota limits and current usage.

faq.question: Can it request quota increases?
faq.answer: It explains the workflow. Actual requests require suitable Azure permissions and operator approval.

faq.question: Why does it prefer Azure CLI?
faq.answer: The skill states that Azure CLI gives clearer quota results than portal or REST fallback paths.

faq.question: Does No Limit mean unlimited capacity?
faq.answer: No. The skill warns that No Limit can mean the quota API lacks support for that resource.

faq.question: Can Claude Code or Codex use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
